ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311737 covers spring roll as a general class; no specific Vietnamese fresh rice-paper roll entry exists. Closest parent match used.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard serving is 4 fresh spring rolls with peanut dipping sauce, weighing approximately 300 g in total.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Spring rolls, fresh, with shrimp and pork (composite estimate); supplemented by FAO/INFOODS Southeast Asian Food Composition Table for regional ingredient cross-check
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for Vietnamese fresh spring rolls (Gỏi Cuốn) exists in USDA FoodData Central or Singapore HPB Foodo. Values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for rice paper, cooked shrimp, pork belly, rice vermicelli, lettuce, fresh herbs, and peanut sauce, cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA table entries for similar Vietnamese mixed rolls. Input values (energy 150 kcal, protein 8.33 g, fat 6.67 g, carbohydrate 15.0 g per 100 g) are consistent with this composite estimate and are retained. Micronutrient values are carried from the input where plausible and flagged as estimated.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — rice vermicelli and rice paper analogous estimate
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. Diabetes Care 2008
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Vietnamese fresh spring rolls (Gỏi Cuốn) in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI estimated at 53 (low) based on the dominant carbohydrate sources: rice vermicelli (GI ~53, University of Sydney GI Database) and rice paper (GI ~52–54, analogous to rice-based products). The protein from shrimp and pork, dietary fibre from fresh herbs and vegetables, and fat from peanut sauce further moderate the glycaemic response. Glycemic load per serving calculated as: GI (53) × carbohydrate per serving (45 g from 300 g × 15 g/100 g) / 100 = 23.9.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 267 mg/100 g; a 300 g serving delivers approximately 800 mg sodium, representing 35–40% of the 2,000 mg daily limit, largely from peanut dipping sauce containing hoisin.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should request peanut sauce on the side and use sparingly, or ask for a reduced-sodium version, to keep the per-serving sodium contribution below 400 mg.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Moderate carbohydrate load (15 g/100 g; ~45 g per 300 g serving) from rice paper and rice vermicelli, partially offset by protein (8.33 g/100 g), dietary fibre (1.33 g/100 g), and fat slowing gastric emptying; estimated GI 53 (low).
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes may consume 2–4 rolls as a controlled portion, pairing with additional non-starchy vegetables and limiting peanut sauce to reduce added sugars from hoisin.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat is low at 2.0 g/100 g and trans fat is negligible at 0.03 g/100 g; however, cholesterol is 50 mg/100 g (150 mg per serving) from shrimp and pork belly, and sodium is borderline elevated.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, limit to one serving of 4 rolls, request reduced peanut sauce to lower sodium and saturated fat, and favour the shrimp component over pork belly to reduce dietary cholesterol intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Pork belly contributes arachidonic acid (omega-6), which may amplify inflammatory pathways; however, shrimp provides omega-3 fatty acids and fresh herbs supply anti-inflammatory polyphenols.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Individuals with rheumatoid arthritis should request substitution of pork belly with extra shrimp and monitor personal response to chili in the peanut sauce, which is a nightshade.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Gỏi Cuốn, known in English as Vietnamese fresh spring rolls or summer rolls, is a classic Vietnamese appetiser consisting of rice paper wrappers filled with sliced pork belly, whole or halved shrimp, rice vermicelli noodles, crisp lettuce, and aromatic fresh herbs such as mint, Thai basil, and coriander. Unlike fried spring rolls, Gỏi Cuốn is assembled cold and served immediately, giving it a light, translucent appearance and a fresh, clean flavour profile with contrasting textures — chewy rice paper, tender noodles, crisp vegetables, and succulent seafood. The rolls are traditionally served with a homemade peanut dipping sauce enriched with hoisin, which adds sweetness, umami, and a creamy richness. Nutritionally, a 300 g serving (4 rolls with sauce) provides approximately 450 kcal, 25 g protein, 20 g fat, and 45 g carbohydrate. The estimated glycemic index is 53 (low), making it a relatively moderate-GI food for a mixed dish. Sodium is borderline at 267 mg/100 g, primarily from the peanut dipping sauce. The dish is dairy-free and rich in lean protein from shrimp. It is widely consumed in Vietnam and across Southeast Asia, and is popular in Vietnamese restaurants in Singapore. Clinically, it is suitable for most dietary patterns in controlled portions, with sodium management being the primary consideration for hypertension and cardiovascular disease patients.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for Vietnamese fresh spring rolls (Gỏi Cuốn) exists in any single approved primary source. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA FoodData Central component data for individual ingredients (rice paper, shrimp, pork belly, rice vermicelli, fresh herbs, peanut sauce) and cross-checked against FAO/INFOODS SEA table entries for similar mixed Vietnamese rolls. The input values from the GrabFood-sourced record are consistent with this composite estimate and have been retained. GI is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli GI from the University of Sydney GI Database. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid profile, B-vitamin panel, fat-soluble vitamins, and moisture data for this composite Vietnamese fresh spring roll preparation are not available in USDA FoodData Central or FAO/INFOODS SEA table as a single composite entry as of 2026. trans_fat_g is 0.03 g/100 g (trace level), retained from input and consistent with minor trans-fat presence in pork belly. All core macros (protein_g 8.33, fat_g 6.67, carbohydrate_g 15.0) are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for this dish. Halal status is marked false as pork belly is a primary ingredient. GI of 53 is an analogous estimate based on rice vermicelli (GI 53, University of Sydney GI Database); no direct GI measurement for Gỏi Cuốn exists in approved sources. Glycemic load per serving = 53 × 45 / 100 = 23.9 (high GL category). Vietnamese cuisine: Vietnamese local name (Gỏi Cuốn) is mandatory and present. This dish is Vietnamese in origin but served in Singapore; Singaporean multilingual requirements (Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) are not mandatory as the cuisine is Vietnamese, not Singaporean. Chinese aliases are included as a courtesy given the Singapore serving context.
